journal_entry.description: Discounting of 90-day promissory note at bank
journal_entry.rationale: Unable to determine correct accounting treatment without knowing the terms of the discounting arrangement.

disambiguator_responses.reason: IFRS 9 requires derecognition analysis based on transfer of risks and rewards. With recourse, Ford retains credit risk and the transaction is a secured borrowing (debit Cash, credit Loan Payable, Notes Receivable stays on books). Without recourse, Ford transfers substantially all risks and the note is derecognized (debit Cash, debit Loss on Discounting, credit Notes Receivable). These produce structurally different entries with different accounts and amounts. The transaction text does not specify recourse terms, and there is no accounting convention default—this is a contractual fact only the transaction initiator knows.

clarification_questions: If the maker of the promissory note defaults on payment at maturity, is Ford obligated to repay the bank (recourse), or does the bank bear the loss (non-recourse)?

options: Derecognition (sale of receivable) - Ford transfers the note to the bank and removes it from books
Collateralized borrowing (loan secured by receivable) - Ford retains the note and records a liability
ambiguities.clarification_question: When Ford discounted the note at the bank, did Ford transfer all risks and rewards of ownership to the bank (sale), or does Ford retain recourse obligations if the note maker defaults (secured loan)?
ambiguities.why_entry_differs: Sale treatment: debit Cash, debit Loss on Discounting (or credit Gain), credit Notes Receivable. Loan treatment: debit Cash, credit Notes Payable (or Loan Payable), with the note remaining on books as an asset.
ambiguities.why_not_resolved: The term 'discounted at the bank' is ambiguous between two distinct accounting treatments. IFRS 9 requires derecognition analysis based on whether substantially all risks and rewards are transferred. The transaction text does not specify recourse terms, risk transfer, or whether Ford remains liable if the maker defaults. Standard convention does not provide a default - this depends on the specific agreement terms between Ford and the bank.

output.reason: This transaction involves Ford discounting (selling) the note receivable to the bank. On the debit side: (1) Cash increases for the net proceeds received (asset increase), and (2) Loss on discounting increases as an expense for the discount charges deducted by the bank (expense increase). The discount charge represents the difference between the note's face value and the present value at the bank's discount rate, recorded as a financing cost/expense. Two distinct debit lines: one asset increase (cash) and one expense increase (loss/discount expense).

debit_classifier.reason: This transaction involves Ford discounting (selling) the note receivable to the bank. On the debit side: (1) Cash increases for the net proceeds received (asset increase), and (2) Loss on discounting increases as an expense for the discount charges deducted by the bank (expense increase). The discount charge represents the difference between the note's face value and the present value at the bank's discount rate, recorded as a financing cost/expense. Two distinct debit lines: one asset increase (cash) and one expense increase (loss/discount expense).

output.reason: On the credit side: (1) The original trade receivable is removed = asset decrease (the note was received as settlement, so the receivable is cleared). (2) Ford recognizes a loss on discounting = expense decrease is NOT applicable here; instead, the loss is a debit-side expense increase. The credit side only includes the removal of the note receivable (which replaced the trade receivable). Since the note is discounted with recourse or sold, the note receivable account is credited = asset decrease. Only one credit line: the note receivable leaving Ford's books.

credit_classifier.reason: On the credit side: (1) The original trade receivable is removed = asset decrease (the note was received as settlement, so the receivable is cleared). (2) Ford recognizes a loss on discounting = expense decrease is NOT applicable here; instead, the loss is a debit-side expense increase. The credit side only includes the removal of the note receivable (which replaced the trade receivable). Since the note is discounted with recourse or sold, the note receivable account is credited = asset decrease. Only one credit line: the note receivable leaving Ford's books.
